title: Mole Removal: When and How to Proceed
content:
Understanding Moles and Removal Indications

Moles, or nevi, are common benign skin lesions that appear as brown, black, or skin-colored spots on the body. While most moles are harmless, they may be removed for cosmetic reasons, functional concerns, or diagnostic evaluation to rule out malignancy.

The decision to remove a mole should be guided by clinical assessment. Dermatologists evaluate moles using the ABCDE criteria: Asymmetry, Border irregularity, Color variation, Diameter greater than 6mm, and Evolution or change. Any mole demonstrating these features warrants removal and histopathological examination.

Clinical Indications for Removal

  • Cosmetic concerns—moles in visible or bothersome locations
  • Diagnostic uncertainty—atypical appearance requiring histology
  • Functional impairment—moles in areas subject to trauma or friction
  • Patient anxiety or concern regarding malignant potential
  • Baseline mole removal for surveillance purposes in high-risk patients

Removal Techniques

Dermatologists employ several techniques for mole removal, each with specific advantages and limitations.

Excision with Primary Closure

Surgical excision remains the gold standard for moles, particularly when histopathological evaluation is required. An elliptical incision is made around the mole, typically with a 1-3mm margin depending on clinical concern. The specimen is removed in its entirety and submitted for pathology. The wound is closed with absorbable and non-absorbable sutures, resulting in a linear scar. This technique is ideal for pigmented lesions where malignancy cannot be clinically excluded.

Shave Removal

Shave removal, performed using a scalpel or dermablade, is appropriate for pedunculated moles or those with minimal dermal component. The lesion is anesthetized and shaved flush with the surrounding skin. While this technique produces minimal scarring, it does not allow complete histopathological evaluation if deep dermal component exists. Risk of recurrence exists if dermal nevi remain.

Cryotherapy

Liquid nitrogen cryotherapy can be employed for select benign moles, though it does not yield a specimen for histology. This method is best reserved for small, clearly benign lesions. Multiple freeze-thaw cycles may be necessary, and depigmentation or hyperpigmentation can occur post-treatment.

Complications and Outcomes

Proper surgical technique minimizes complications. Expected outcomes include transient erythema, suture marks if sutures are placed, and linear scar formation with excision. Infection, though uncommon with appropriate sterile technique, should be managed with oral antibiotics. Recurrence of junctional or intradermal nevi can occur after shave removal if deep nests remain.

Patients should be counseled that excision produces the most reliable diagnostic and cosmetic outcomes, particularly for lesions of uncertain clinical significance.
meta_description: Comprehensive guide to mole removal indications and techniques including excision, shave removal, and cryotherapy options.

title: Epidermoid Cyst Excision: Technique and Management
content:
Epidermoid Cysts: Definition and Clinical Presentation

Epidermoid cysts, formerly called sebaceous cysts, are benign, keratinous lesions that develop from epithelial tissue. They present as solitary or multiple flesh-colored to yellowish nodules, often with a central punctum. While benign, they warrant removal when cosmetically unacceptable, infected, or when diagnostic confirmation is necessary.

Pre-operative Assessment

Clinical examination typically suffices for diagnosis. The cyst should be assessed for size, location, mobility, and any signs of infection or inflammation. If cyst rupture has occurred, surrounding erythema and drainage may be present. Pre-operative imaging is generally unnecessary unless the lesion is large or located in a critical area.

Surgical Technique

Standard Excision Method

After administering local anesthesia with lidocaine and epinephrine, a small incision is made over the cyst punctum or along the longitudinal axis. The cyst wall should be identified and carefully dissected from surrounding tissue. Complete removal of the cyst wall is essential; incomplete removal leaves the lining intact and allows recurrence.

The entire cyst content and wall are removed as a unified specimen. Gentle traction aids dissection without rupturing the delicate cyst wall. Once completely extirpated, the cavity is inspected for completeness. The wound is then irrigated and closed with absorbable sutures in the deeper layer and non-absorbable sutures in the skin.

Minimal-Incision Technique

For smaller cysts, a minimal-incision approach can be employed. A small puncture incision is made, and the cyst is carefully expressed and removed. This technique reduces scarring but carries increased risk of incomplete wall removal and recurrence if not performed meticulously.

Management of Inflamed or Infected Cysts

Acutely infected cysts should not be immediately excised. Initial management includes incision and drainage, warm compresses, and antibiotics if cellulitis is present. Definitive excision should be deferred until inflammation resolves, typically 4-6 weeks after drainage. This allows the surrounding tissue inflammation to subside and reduces operative complexity.

Post-operative Care and Complications

Sutures are typically removed at 7-10 days depending on location. Patients should keep the wound clean and dry. Infection is rare with proper sterile technique but should be managed with oral antibiotics. Recurrence rates are minimized with complete cyst wall removal, occurring in less than 5% of cases when excision is performed properly.
meta_description: Detailed surgical technique for epidermoid cyst removal including standard excision, minimal-incision approach, and management.

title: Pilar Cyst Removal: Surgical Approach
content:
Pilar Cysts: Characteristics and Clinical Features

Pilar cysts, also known as trichilemmal cysts, are common benign lesions that arise from the outer root sheath of hair follicles. They typically occur on the scalp and appear as firm, mobile nodules. Unlike epidermoid cysts, pilar cysts have a thicker, more fibrous wall and contain a homogeneous keratinous material without a defined punctum.

Indications for Removal

Removal is indicated for cosmetic concerns, large size that may cause discomfort or hair loss, recurrent inflammation, or patient preference. Pilar cysts do not malignantly transform, so removal is purely elective in most cases.

Surgical Excision Technique

Scalp Location Considerations

The scalp's rich vascular supply and hair-bearing nature require modified surgical approach. A linear incision is made over the cyst, typically in a superior-inferior orientation to allow favorable scar orientation with hair growth pattern. Local anesthesia with epinephrine is essential for hemostasis.

Cyst Removal Steps

Once anesthetized, the incision is made through skin and underlying tissue. Careful dissection along the cyst capsule allows for complete removal while minimizing damage to surrounding healthy tissue. The dense fibrous wall of pilar cysts requires careful handling to avoid rupture.

After complete extraction, the wound bed is irrigated. Hair follicles and sebaceous glands that are not damaged should be preserved. Closure is performed with interrupted absorbable sutures in the deeper layers and non-absorbable sutures in the skin, taking care to avoid hair follicles during suture placement.

Multiple Cyst Management

Patients with multiple pilar cysts can have all lesions removed in a single procedure if they are in proximity. This reduces overall operative time and wound healing period. However, large numbers of cysts may warrant staged procedures to minimize operative time and wound burden.

Post-operative Care on Scalp

Wound care is critical on the scalp due to the environment and hair. Patients should keep the area clean and dry, avoiding shampooing the immediate wound area until suture removal. Sutures are removed at 10-14 days depending on healing. The risk of infection on the scalp is higher than other sites, requiring vigilant monitoring.

Recurrence Rates

Complete cyst wall removal minimizes recurrence, with rates typically less than 5%. Recurrent cysts should be managed with repeat excision, ensuring complete capsule removal.
meta_description: Surgical management of pilar cysts including scalp considerations, technique, and post-operative care instructions.

title: Lipoma Removal: Surgical Extraction Techniques
content:
Lipomas: Definition and Clinical Characteristics

Lipomas are benign, slow-growing tumors composed of mature adipose tissue. They present as soft, mobile, painless subcutaneous nodules that can occur anywhere on the body. While benign, they warrant removal when cosmetically concerning, rapidly enlarging, or causing functional impairment.

Pre-operative Evaluation

Clinical examination with palpation determines the size, depth, and mobility of the lipoma. Imaging with ultrasound or MRI may be warranted for large lesions, deep lipomas, or those in sensitive anatomical locations. This ensures proper surgical planning and identifies any relationship to underlying structures.

Surgical Techniques for Removal

Open Excision with Primary Closure

Open excision remains the standard for most lipomas. After local anesthesia with epinephrine, an incision is made directly over the lesion or in a favorable skin tension line. The lipoma capsule is carefully identified and the lesion is mobilized using gentle traction and scissors dissection along the plane between capsule and surrounding tissue.

Blunt dissection with a periosteal elevator or similar instrument can facilitate removal. Once the entire lesion is freed, it is extracted and the wound is irrigated. Hemostasis is achieved with pressure and electrocautery as needed. The incision is closed with absorbable sutures in deeper layers and non-absorbable sutures in skin.

Minimal-Incision Liposuction Technique

For smaller superficial lipomas, a minimal-incision approach using suction-assisted lipectomy can be effective. A small incision is made and a small-diameter cannula is used to aspirate and remove the lipoma tissue. This technique reduces scarring but requires experience to ensure complete removal and carries higher recurrence risk if not thoroughly performed.

Tumescent Technique

Large lipomas may benefit from tumescent anesthesia, which involves infiltration of dilute epinephrine and lidocaine solution throughout the lesion and surrounding tissue. This provides profound anesthesia, hemostasis, and reduces blood loss. The lipoma can then be removed with conventional open excision using the tumescent field.

Size Considerations

Small lipomas (less than 2cm) can be removed with minimal scars using closed technique. Large lipomas (greater than 5cm) often require larger incisions and may need staged procedures if they are extremely large. The location and depth affect the approach—deep intramuscular lipomas may require referral to surgical colleagues.

Complications and Outcomes

Hematoma and seroma can occur postoperatively but usually resolve without intervention. Nerve injury is rare with careful technique. Incomplete removal leads to recurrence in 1-5% of cases. Patients should be counseled about expected scar appearance and healing timeline.
meta_description: Complete guide to lipoma surgical removal including open excision, minimal-incision techniques, and post-operative management.

title: Skin Tag Removal Methods and Techniques
content:
Skin Tags: Overview and Epidemiology

Skin tags (acrochordons) are common benign lesions composed of loose fibrous tissue and blood vessels. They typically appear as small, flesh-colored or pigmented pendulous growths in skin fold areas. While completely benign, they are frequently removed for cosmetic reasons and to prevent irritation from friction or trauma.

Removal Indications

  • Cosmetic concerns, particularly on visible body areas
  • Recurrent irritation or bleeding from trauma
  • Functional impairment due to location or size
  • Patient preference for removal

Removal Techniques

Cryotherapy

Liquid nitrogen application is a simple, effective method for small to moderate skin tags. The lesion is identified and liquid nitrogen is applied using a cotton applicator or direct spray method for 10-30 seconds. A single or double freeze-thaw cycle may be used. The tag typically separates within 1-3 weeks. Hypopigmentation may occur, particularly in darker-skinned individuals.

Electrosurgery

Electrocautery using radiofrequency energy can remove skin tags with minimal bleeding. The tag is grasped with forceps or a hemostat, and the electrocautery device is applied to cauterize the base. The tag is then removed. This technique provides hemostasis and a sealed wound but may cause mild scarring or post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation.

Surgical Excision

For larger pedunculated tags, direct excision with scissors under local anesthesia is quick and effective. After anesthesia, the tag is grasped, and the base is cut with scissors or scalpel. Hemostasis is achieved with pressure or electrocautery. No sutures are typically required. This method allows for histopathology if diagnostic uncertainty exists.

Ligation

For pedunculated tags, ligation using surgical thread or suture material creates an ischemic environment leading to auto-amputation. The suture is tied tightly around the tag base, and the tag typically falls off within 1-2 weeks. This technique minimizes bleeding and scarring but requires patient acceptance of delayed removal.

Anesthesia Considerations

Most skin tag removal requires minimal anesthesia. Local infiltration of lidocaine is used for surgical excision and electrosurgery. Cryotherapy and ligation may be performed without anesthesia for very small tags.

Post-operative Care

Minimal wound care is usually sufficient. Patients should keep the area clean and dry. If sutures are placed, they are removed at 7-10 days. Complications are rare but may include mild bleeding, infection, or recurrence at the same site.
meta_description: Comprehensive review of skin tag removal methods including cryotherapy, electrosurgery, excision, and ligation techniques.

title: Wart Surgery: When Topical Treatment Fails
content:
Warts: Overview and Treatment Escalation

Warts are common viral skin growths caused by human papillomavirus (HPV) infection. While most respond to topical treatment with salicylic acid, imiquimod, or cryotherapy, some warts demonstrate resistance or recurrence. Surgical intervention becomes necessary when conservative approaches fail or for warts in locations where topical therapy is impractical.

Indications for Surgical Intervention

  • Failure of topical therapy after 4-6 weeks of appropriate treatment
  • Recurrent warts despite multiple treatment courses
  • Warts in difficult locations (palms, soles, nail areas)
  • Plantar warts causing pain or functional limitation
  • Immunocompromised patients with rapidly proliferating warts
  • Patient preference for definitive treatment

Surgical Techniques

Electrosurgery and Curettage

Electrocautery combined with curettage is effective for most warts. After local anesthesia, the wart is debrided with a dermal curette, removing the hyperkeratotic surface. Electrocautery is then used to ablate the remaining wart tissue and seal blood vessels. This technique provides hemostasis and reduces recurrence by destroying the viral-infected tissue base.

Surgical Excision

Warts can be surgically excised with a scalpel or scissors under local anesthesia. The wart is removed with a small margin of normal tissue, and the wound is closed with sutures or left to heal by secondary intention depending on size and location. Histopathology can be obtained if diagnostic uncertainty exists, though this is rarely necessary for typical warts.

Laser Surgery

CO2 laser ablation effectively vaporizes wart tissue. The laser creates controlled thermal injury that removes infected tissue while minimizing damage to surrounding normal skin. Multiple passes may be necessary to achieve complete ablation. Laser treatment is particularly useful for stubborn warts but carries higher cost and requires specialized equipment.

Cryotherapy

Aggressive liquid nitrogen cryotherapy with multiple freeze-thaw cycles can be curative for resistant warts when properly applied. Cryotherapy is non-invasive and can be repeated as needed, though multiple treatment sessions are often required for complete resolution.

Special Considerations for Location

Plantar Warts

Plantar warts are particularly resistant due to weight-bearing location and thick surrounding callus. Electrosurgery combined with aggressive curettage is effective. Complete removal of keratinous material and wart tissue is essential. Recurrence rates are higher than non-plantar warts.

Periungual Warts

Warts around and under nails require careful technique to avoid nail bed damage. Partial nail avulsion may be necessary to access and remove subungual wart tissue. Scarring of the nail bed can occur, potentially causing permanent nail dystrophy.

Post-operative Care and Recurrence

After surgical removal, wound care depends on the technique used. If sutures are placed, removal is at 7-10 days. Electrocautery and laser wounds heal by secondary intention. Recurrence can occur if even small amounts of viral-infected tissue remain, occurring in 5-20% of surgically treated warts depending on location and treatment method.
meta_description: Surgical approaches to resistant warts including electrosurgery, excision, laser, and special considerations for location.

title: Partial Nail Avulsion: Technique and Indications
content:
Nail Avulsion: Overview and Indications

Nail avulsion is a surgical procedure in which the nail plate is removed, with or without removal of the nail matrix. Partial nail avulsion (PNA) removes a lateral or central portion of the nail plate and its underlying matrix, while preserving a portion of the nail. This procedure is indicated for various nail pathologies including onychomycosis, nail bed tumors, and chronic paronychia.

Clinical Indications for Partial Avulsion

  • Onychomycosis affecting only a portion of the nail
  • Nail bed squamous cell carcinoma or other tumors
  • Chronic paronychia unresponsive to conservative treatment
  • Ingrown toenails (lateral nail avulsion)
  • Subungual hematoma with underlying nail bed injury
  • Nail bed granulation tissue or hypertrophic changes

Surgical Technique for Partial Nail Avulsion

Anesthesia

Digital block anesthesia is essential, providing complete pain control for the digit. A tourniquet is applied to provide a bloodless field and improve visualization. The nail is soaked in saline for 2-3 minutes to soften the nail plate-nail bed interface.

Nail Plate Removal

For lateral partial avulsion (commonly for ingrown nails), the lateral nail plate is elevated using a nail elevator along the lateral nail plate margin. The plate is carefully lifted from the nail bed, separating along the natural cleavage plane. Curved scissors or a surgical blade can be used to divide the nail plate vertically to separate the lateral portion from the remainder.

Matrix Removal

If nail matrix destruction is planned to prevent regrowth, the matrix tissue (the growth center of the nail) is identified under the nail fold. Chemical ablation using phenol 88% on gauze soaked pads is applied for 1-2 minutes, followed by thorough irrigation with alcohol and saline. Alternatively, electrocautery or CO2 laser can be used for matrix ablation.

Closure

After matrix treatment or hemostasis, the wound is closed with absorbable sutures if a raw surface remains. The nail fold is then sutured if necessary. Typically, no dressing is required, though antibiotic ointment and a protective bandage provide patient comfort.

Considerations for Matrix Preservation

If the goal is to preserve nail regrowth, the matrix should not be treated with chemical or thermal ablation. Simply removing the nail plate allows regrowth of a new nail. This approach is useful when the pathology is superficial and not inherent to the matrix.

Post-operative Care

Sutures are removed at 2-3 weeks. Patients should keep the digit clean and dry. The nail takes 3-4 months to completely regrow if the matrix is preserved. If matrix was ablated, no nail regrowth occurs in that area, creating a narrowed nail plate.

Complications

Regrowth is possible if matrix destruction is incomplete. Nail dystrophy can occur if the matrix is partially damaged. Infection is uncommon with appropriate sterile technique. Permanent widening of the matrix-free nail fold may occur if wide areas of matrix are removed.
meta_description: Comprehensive guide to partial nail avulsion including indications, surgical technique, matrix management, and post-operative care.

title: Total Nail Avulsion: Procedure and Outcomes
content:
Total Nail Avulsion: Definition and Clinical Context

Total nail avulsion (TNA) involves complete removal of the nail plate, and often the underlying nail matrix, from the nail bed. This definitive procedure is indicated for severe nail pathology involving the entire nail unit where preservation of the nail is not the goal. Common indications include severe onychomycosis with nail dystrophy, extensive nail bed involvement of malignancy, and chronic paronychia unresponsive to less invasive treatments.

Indications for Total Avulsion

  • Severe onychomycosis with extensive nail plate and nail bed involvement
  • Nail bed malignancy requiring complete nail unit removal for adequate surgical margins
  • Severe nail dystrophy with destruction of the nail matrix
  • Chronic paronychia with nail matrix involvement
  • Nail bed injury with loss of matrix function
  • Nail unit trauma with non-salvageable nail apparatus

Pre-operative Preparation

For anticipated onychomycosis treatment, fungal culture may be performed prior to surgery for organism identification. Imaging is obtained for tumors to assess depth and extent. Digital block anesthesia and tourniquet application provide appropriate pain control and hemostasis.

Surgical Technique

Nail Plate Removal

The entire nail plate is carefully elevated from the nail bed using a nail elevator or periosteal elevator, working from the distal free edge proximally under the nail fold. The nail plate is separated from the nail bed along the natural cleavage plane. Care is taken to avoid creating raw areas that would require complex closure.

Complete Matrix Removal

For total nail avulsion with matrix removal, the underlying nail matrix must be completely destroyed to prevent nail regrowth. The matrix consists of the dorsal (superficial) and ventral (deep) portions. Chemical ablation using phenol 88% is the traditional method. Phenol is applied on gauze soaked pads placed over the entire nail bed for 2-3 minutes.

Alternative ablation methods include electrocautery, which allows controlled thermal destruction of matrix tissue, and CO2 laser ablation, which provides precise tissue destruction. All methods are followed by thorough irrigation with alcohol and saline to remove any caustic material.

Wound Management

After complete nail plate and matrix removal, the nail bed is left as a raw surface. Hemostasis is achieved with pressure, electrocautery, or tourniquets. Some surgeons place antibiotic-impregnated gauze or allow the wound to heal by secondary intention. The wound epithelializes over 2-4 weeks.

Pathology Handling

When total nail avulsion is performed for suspected malignancy, the entire nail apparatus is submitted for histopathological examination. The specimen is oriented for accurate margin assessment and tumor depth evaluation.

Post-operative Outcomes

After total nail avulsion with matrix ablation, the nail bed becomes a smooth hairless area that no longer produces nail. This creates a cosmetic change and loss of protective function of the nail. Patients should be counseled about this permanent alteration prior to surgery.

Complications and Prevention

Incomplete matrix removal leads to nail regrowth, sometimes in abnormal patterns. Phenol toxicity is avoided through proper application and irrigation technique. Wound infection can occur but is preventable with sterile technique. Nail bed scarring may result from healing, potentially affecting finger dexterity if extensive.
meta_description: Complete guide to total nail avulsion including indications, surgical technique, matrix ablation methods, and post-operative management.

title: Matricectomy: Chemical and Thermal Matrix Ablation
content:
Matricectomy: Definition and Surgical Goal

Matricectomy is the surgical ablation or removal of the nail matrix, the germinal tissue responsible for nail production. This procedure is performed in conjunction with nail plate avulsion when permanent prevention of nail regrowth is desired. The nail matrix consists of dorsal and ventral portions producing the hard nail plate and softer nail bed layers respectively.

Indications for Matricectomy

  • Ingrown toenails with chronic inflammation unresponsive to conservative care
  • Prevention of nail regrowth after onychomycosis treatment
  • Prevention of nail regrowth after nail bed tumor removal
  • Nail dystrophy from chronic paronychia with matrix involvement
  • Recurrent nail pathology affecting the nail unit

Chemical Matricectomy

Phenol Application

Phenol 88% is the most commonly used chemical agent for matrix ablation. After nail plate avulsion under tourniquet control, phenol is applied on gauze pads directly to the matrix area for 1-3 minutes depending on the size of area being treated. The contact time must be sufficient to achieve complete matrix destruction without being excessively prolonged.

Application Technique

Precise application is critical to avoid phenol contact with non-target tissues. Gauze soaked in 88% phenol is placed directly over the matrix area, and the surrounding nail bed and nail fold are protected with mineral oil or vaseline to prevent unintended chemical burn. After the desired contact time, the phenol is thoroughly irrigated away with isopropyl alcohol followed by copious saline irrigation.

Advantages and Limitations

Phenol is inexpensive and effective, with success rates exceeding 95% in most series. However, potential systemic toxicity exists if absorbed in large quantities, though this is rare with appropriate localized application. Residual chemical sensation may occur if irrigation is incomplete.

Thermal Matricectomy

Electrocautery

Electrocautery can be used for matrix ablation, providing controlled thermal destruction. After nail plate removal, the electrocautery device is applied directly to the nail matrix tissue with careful attention to depth and extent of destruction. Multiple passes may be necessary to ensure complete matrix elimination.

CO2 Laser Matricectomy

CO2 laser provides precise ablation of matrix tissue with minimal collateral thermal damage. The laser is directed at the matrix area under tourniquet control with magnification. Multiple passes may be required for complete destruction. This method provides excellent visibility and controllable depth of ablation.

Comparison of Ablation Methods

Success Rates

All three methods demonstrate high success rates (>95%) when properly applied. Phenol is most commonly used and well-studied. Electrocautery is readily available in most surgical settings. Laser offers enhanced precision but requires specialized equipment.

Cost Considerations

Phenol is the least expensive option. Electrocautery requires standard surgical equipment most facilities possess. Laser matricectomy is more costly but may reduce recurrence rates in high-risk cases.

Post-operative Care

Sutures are removed at 2-3 weeks. The nail bed epithelializes over 3-4 weeks. Patients should keep the area clean and dry. Antibiotic ointment is applied for comfort and infection prevention.

Complications and Outcomes

Incomplete ablation leads to nail regrowth, sometimes in abnormal patterns. Nail bed scarring may occur, potentially affecting appearance. Permanent loss of nail in the affected area is the expected outcome. Most patients are satisfied with the resolution of symptoms.
meta_description: Comprehensive guide to matricectomy including phenol, electrocautery, and laser ablation techniques with success rates and outcomes.

title: Dermabrasion: Technique and Clinical Applications
content:
Dermabrasion: Definition and Mechanism

Dermabrasion is a mechanical resurfacing procedure that uses rotating abrasive surfaces to remove superficial skin layers. By removing epidermis and superficial dermis, dermabrasion reduces the appearance of scars, fine wrinkles, and other skin surface irregularities. The technique relies on controlled removal of damaged tissue to promote healing and remodeling.

Clinical Indications

  • Facial acne scars, particularly rolling and boxcar scars
  • Traumatic scars with superficial or mid-dermal involvement
  • Wrinkles and fine lines from sun damage or aging
  • Post-surgical scars in early healing phase
  • Removal of superficial skin lesions or tattoos
  • Pigmentary irregularities from burns or trauma

Patient Selection and Pre-operative Evaluation

Ideal candidates are those with facial scars that are superficial to mid-dermal in depth. Patients with darker skin types should be counseled about higher risk of post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation and hypopigmentation. Those with keloid history or poor wound healing capacity are poor candidates. Pre-operative facial photography documents baseline appearance.

Dermabrasion Technique

Anesthesia and Preparation

Dermabrasion is performed under general anesthesia or conscious sedation with local anesthesia. The face is cleaned thoroughly and marked to delineate treatment areas. A refrigerant spray is often applied to the skin to firm the dermal tissue and facilitate uniform abrasion.

Abrading Process

A rotating abrader with various grit sizes is used, with coarser grits for initial abrasion and finer grits for finishing. The device is moved slowly and steadily across the treatment area with light pressure, following natural contours and skin tension lines. The goal is uniform abrasion to the appropriate depth—typically removing epidermis and reaching the papillary or superficial reticular dermis.

Periorbital and perioral areas require extra caution due to thin skin and proximity to structures. The neck and cheeks are abraded with consistent pressure to avoid depressions or irregularities. Feathering of edges ensures smooth transition to untreated skin.

Depth Control

Proper depth control is essential to achieve the desired result without excessive injury. Bleeding indicates entry into the dermis—small capillary oozing is expected. Depth is assessed by visual landmarks and by the sensation of resistance. Over-abrasion increases scarring risk and prolongs healing.

Post-operative Care

Immediately after dermabrasion, the abraded surface is treated with antibiotic ointment and a protective dressing. Open-wound care with frequent cleansing and ointment application is necessary for 2-3 weeks until re-epithelialization is complete. Patients must scrupulously avoid sun exposure for months to years to prevent post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation.

Results and Complications

Improvement in scar appearance typically becomes apparent at 2-3 months as edema resolves and collagen remodeling progresses. Final results are evident at 6-12 months. Post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation and hypopigmentation are the most common complications, particularly in darker skin types. Milia (small white bumps) may develop transiently. Scarring from over-aggressive abrasion can worsen appearance if excessive depth is achieved.
meta_description: Complete guide to dermabrasion including technique, patient selection, depth control, post-operative care, and expected outcomes.

title: Scar Revision Surgery: Techniques and Outcomes
content:
Scar Revision: Goals and Principles

Scar revision surgery aims to improve the appearance of scars through various surgical and non-surgical techniques. The goals include improving color, texture, depth, width, and directional alignment. Effective scar revision requires understanding the scar pathophysiology and selecting appropriate techniques for each individual scar.

Scar Assessment

Scar Classification

Scars are classified as normotrophic (flat, in-plane with skin), hypertrophic (raised and thick), atrophic (depressed below skin surface), or keloid (extending beyond original injury boundaries). Treatment approach depends on scar type and depth.

Timing Considerations

Most scars mature over 12-18 months as collagen remodeling progresses. Revision should generally be deferred until maturation is complete. Early revision within 3-6 months of injury may be appropriate for poorly aligned scars or those that are severely cosmetically unacceptable.

Revision Techniques

Surgical Excision and Re-closure

For wide or poorly oriented scars, excision followed by re-closure along a more favorable tension line improves appearance. The scar is excised with minimal margins, and the wound is closed meticulously with attention to wound edge eversion and tension distribution. Multiple smaller scars are preferable to one large scar in many locations.

Z-plasty

Z-plasty is a useful technique for scars that restrict motion or are oriented contrary to skin tension lines. The scar is excised in the shape of a Z, with the central portion aligned with the scar and the lateral limbs at 60-degree angles. Transposition of the flaps redirects the scar orientation, reducing tension and improving appearance. Multiple Z-plasties can be used for longer scars.

W-plasty and Serial Revisions

W-plasty and similar techniques create multiple small irregular portions along the scar line rather than a single straight line. This breaks up the visual line of the scar, making it less noticeable. Multiple staged procedures may be necessary to achieve optimal results.

Subcutaneous Tissue Elevation

For depressed atrophic scars, elevation of the skin through subcutaneous scoring or suspension techniques can improve depression. Absorbable or non-absorbable sutures placed through the scar dermis and attached to deeper tissues or skin provide elevation. This technique is useful for rolling scars and minor atrophic depressions.

Adjunctive and Non-surgical Treatments

Laser Resurfacing

Fractional and ablative lasers improve scar appearance by inducing controlled collagen remodeling and removing surface irregularities. Multiple treatments are often necessary. Risks include post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation and permanent hypopigmentation.

Dermal Fillers

Temporary fillers such as hyaluronic acid can elevate depressed scars, improving appearance temporarily. Repeated treatments are necessary as fillers resorb over months. This approach is useful for patients unwilling to undergo surgery or as adjunctive treatment.

Microneedling

Microneedling induces collagen production through controlled microtrauma, improving scar appearance over time. Multiple treatments are needed, typically spaced 4-6 weeks apart. Results are modest but accumulate over multiple sessions.

Expected Outcomes

Scar revision achieves improvement rather than complete elimination. Most patients experience significant improvement in scar appearance, with results most apparent at 3-6 months as collagen remodeling occurs. Complete scar elimination is not achievable, as the mark of injury always remains to some degree.
meta_description: Comprehensive guide to scar revision including excision, Z-plasty, laser, fillers, and microneedling techniques with outcomes.

title: Keloid Excision with Adjuvant Therapy
content:
Keloids: Pathophysiology and Treatment Challenge

Keloids are abnormal scars that extend beyond the original injury boundaries, resulting from aberrant collagen deposition and excessive inflammatory response. Unlike hypertrophic scars which remain within original injury margins, keloids continue growing indefinitely without treatment. Surgical excision alone has recurrence rates of 40-50%, necessitating adjuvant therapies to improve outcomes.

Risk Factors and Patient Assessment

Higher risk for keloid formation exists in darker-skinned individuals, those with family history, and those with injuries in predisposed areas (shoulders, chest, earlobes). Keloid location affects treatment strategy—some sites have higher recurrence rates. Pre-operative photography and scar size documentation guide treatment planning.

Surgical Excision Technique

Primary Excision

The keloid is excised with margins that remain within the original scar borders or planned surgical site. Margins should not extend beyond necessary tissue. The wound is closed with intradermal sutures and skin closure, with meticulous attention to tension distribution. Excessive tension on closure must be avoided as high tension promotes keloid recurrence.

Wound Closure Methods

Intradermal closure with absorbable sutures reduces tension on the skin surface. Skin closure is typically performed with fine non-absorbable sutures or tissue adhesive. Sutures are removed at 3-4 weeks. Some surgeons prefer delayed closure or skin grafting to reduce tension, particularly for large keloids.

Adjuvant Therapies to Reduce Recurrence

Intralesional Corticosteroids

Intralesional corticosteroid injection immediately after excision or at the first post-operative visit reduces keloid recurrence. Triamcinolone 40 mg/mL is injected directly into the healing scar tissue at 4-6 week intervals. This disrupts collagen deposition and inflammatory response. Multiple injections are often necessary over 6-12 months.

Pressure Garments

Continuous pressure garment application over the healing scar provides mechanical suppression of collagen synthesis. Garments are worn for 12-24 hours daily for 3-6 months. While evidence for efficacy is moderate, they are non-invasive and commonly recommended.

Silicone Gel Sheeting

Silicone gel sheets applied to the scar provide occlusion, hydration, and potential modification of collagen deposition. Sheets are worn for 12 hours daily for several months. This approach is well-tolerated and safe but evidence for efficacy in keloid prevention is mixed.

Radiation Therapy

Post-operative radiation (typically 12-15 Gy in 2-3 fractions) significantly reduces keloid recurrence, with some studies reporting recurrence rates less than 10%. However, radiation carries long-term risks and is reserved for severe keloids or recurrent cases. The timing and dosing of radiation are critical—treatment is typically delivered within days after excision.

Laser Therapy

Pulsed dye laser or other lasers can be used post-operatively to reduce redness and flatten remaining scar tissue. Laser works synergistically with other adjuvant therapies. Multiple treatments spaced 4-6 weeks apart are typically necessary.

Combination Approach

Most effective outcomes result from combination therapy: surgical excision followed by intralesional corticosteroids, pressure therapy, and sometimes radiation for aggressive or recurrent keloids. This multimodal approach reduces recurrence rates significantly compared to surgery alone.

Outcomes and Realistic Expectations

With appropriate adjuvant therapy, recurrence rates can be reduced to 20-30%, compared to 40-50% with surgery alone. However, complete prevention is not always achievable. Patients should be counseled about the chronic nature of keloid disease and the need for long-term follow-up and additional treatment if recurrence occurs.
meta_description: Complete guide to keloid excision including surgical technique and adjuvant therapies: steroids, pressure, radiation, and laser.

title: Ear Reconstruction After Mohs Micrographic Surgery
content:
Ear Reconstruction Following Mohs: Challenges and Planning

Mohs micrographic surgery is frequently employed for skin cancers of the ear due to excellent cure rates and tissue preservation. However, Mohs surgery often results in significant defects that require reconstruction to restore ear structure and function. Successful ear reconstruction requires understanding ear anatomy, optimal timing, and appropriate reconstruction techniques.

Defect Assessment

Size and Location Classification

Ear defects are classified by location (helix, antihelix, concha, ear canal, earlobe) and size. Defects are further characterized by depth and involvement of underlying cartilage. The location determines reconstruction approach—cartilage involvement necessitates cartilage grafting or flap reconstruction.

Timing of Reconstruction

Immediate reconstruction at the time of final Mohs stage is ideal when defect size is known and margins are confirmed clear. This reduces total operative time and anesthesia exposure. However, some surgeons prefer staged reconstruction after histopathology is complete to ensure adequate margins.

Reconstruction Techniques

Primary Closure

Small defects involving skin only can often be closed primarily. Tension-free closure using advancement or rotation techniques preserves ear contour. Fine sutures and meticulous technique optimize cosmetic outcome.

Skin Grafting

For larger defects without cartilage involvement, full-thickness skin grafts provide durable reconstruction. Grafts are harvested from post-auricular skin or other areas with good color match. The graft is sutured to the defect edges, and a tie-over bolster provides immobilization and hemostasis for 7-10 days. Graft take rates exceed 95% when proper technique is used.

Cartilage Grafting

When cartilage is exposed or removed, cartilage grafting prevents collapse and deformity. Cartilage can be harvested from the opposite ear (if available), costochondral rib, or tragus. The graft is shaped to restore contour and sutured in place. A skin graft then covers the cartilage graft.

Local Flap Reconstruction

Rotation, advancement, or transposition flaps using adjacent ear and periauricular tissue can reconstruct helix and ear margin defects. These flaps maintain hair-bearing characteristics and sensory innervation better than grafts. Flap design must account for blood supply—the superior temporal artery supplies most ear flaps.

Composite Grafting

For very small defects, particularly of the ear margin, composite grafts combining skin and cartilage from the opposite ear or other sources restore contour and function. However, composite grafts have lower take rates than skin grafts alone due to limited vascular supply.

Post-operative Care and Outcomes

Careful post-operative wound care and protection is essential. Patients should avoid manipulation of the repair. Sutures are removed at 1-2 weeks depending on location. Long-term sun protection prevents recurrence of skin cancer in the remaining ear.

Complications

Graft failure is uncommon with proper technique but results in contraction and deformity. Hematoma under grafts should be aspirated early. Infection is prevented through sterile technique. Secondary deformities may require touch-up procedures.
meta_description: Comprehensive guide to ear reconstruction after Mohs surgery including defect assessment, skin grafts, cartilage grafts, and flap techniques.

title: Nose Reconstruction After Mohs Micrographic Surgery
content:
Nasal Reconstruction: Complexity and Considerations

Nose reconstruction after Mohs micrographic surgery represents one of the most challenging reconstructive problems in dermatologic surgery. The nose has complex three-dimensional anatomy, prominent aesthetic importance, and functional requirements for breathing. Successful reconstruction requires careful planning and mastery of multiple surgical techniques.

Defect Assessment and Classification

Anatomical Zones

The nose is divided into aesthetic subunits: nasal dorsum, sidewalls, tip, columella, ala, and soft triangle. Defects are assessed by size, location within subunits, depth, and involvement of supporting structures (cartilage, bone). Subunit reconstruction principles guide approach—restoration within subunit lines produces superior aesthetic results.

Depth Classification

Superficial defects involve skin only. Intermediate defects involve subcutaneous tissue and may require cartilage support. Full-thickness defects involve mucosa or deeper structures and require complex reconstruction.

Reconstruction Techniques

Primary Closure and Direct Repair

Small defects on the nasal dorsum or sidewall can be closed primarily with careful attention to contour and tension distribution. The nose's curved surface requires design of closure lines that follow natural skin tension lines and aesthetic boundaries.

Skin Grafting

Full-thickness skin grafts are useful for moderate-sized defects. Nasal skin has characteristic color and texture; grafts should be harvested from similar regions—post-auricular, preauricular, or supraclavicular skin provides good color and texture match. Grafts are secured with tie-over bolsters for 7-10 days.

Local Flaps

Rotation, advancement, and transposition flaps using surrounding nasal and facial skin are powerful tools for nasal reconstruction. The nasal dorsum and sidewalls are particularly well-suited to flap reconstruction due to available adjacent tissue.

Nasolabial Flap

The nasolabial flap, based on branches of the superior labial artery, can reconstruct the nasal ala and lower nose defects. The flap is elevated from the cheek and upper lip region and transposed into the nasal defect. This flap provides excellent color and texture match for lower nasal reconstruction.

Forehead Flap

Large nasal defects, particularly involving the nasal tip, often require forehead flap reconstruction. The paramedian forehead flap, based on the supratrochlear artery, provides a robust blood supply and excellent tissue color match. The flap is elevated in a paramedian location, transposed into the nasal defect, and the pedicle is divided at 3 weeks allowing flap inset. This staged approach requires 2-3 procedures but achieves superior aesthetic results.

Cartilage Grafting

Defects involving loss of nasal cartilage or loss of nasal structure require cartilage grafting to restore contour and maintain airway patency. Cartilage is typically harvested from septal cartilage, ear cartilage, or rib cartilage depending on quantity and strength needed. The graft is sculpted to restore nasal contour and sutured securely.

Specific Site Considerations

Alar Reconstruction

Alar defects pose particular functional and aesthetic challenges. Loss of alar support can cause airway obstruction and distortion. Reconstruction requires cartilage grafting with flap coverage to restore both structure and function.

Columellar Reconstruction

The columella provides structural support and defines the nasal dorsum profile. Columellar defects require cartilage-containing reconstruction to restore proper support and aesthetic alignment.

Timing and Staging

Immediate reconstruction at the time of Mohs completion is often preferable when defect is well-defined. However, staged reconstruction may be necessary for very large defects or when flaps require pedicle division (as with forehead flap).

Post-operative Management

Nasal splinting for 1 week protects the reconstruction and maintains contour. Patients should avoid nasal manipulation and strenuous activity. Follow-up visits monitor for complications and assess healing. Secondary refinements may be necessary as edema resolves and scars mature.

Outcomes and Complications

Successful nasal reconstruction restores both aesthetic appearance and nasal function. Common complications include graft failure, flap necrosis, and secondary deformity. However, the rich blood supply of the nasal region generally supports good healing when proper technique is employed.
meta_description: Comprehensive guide to nasal reconstruction after Mohs surgery including defect assessment, skin grafts, flaps, and cartilage grafts.

title: Earlobe Repair: Techniques and Restoration
content:
Earlobe Defects: Etiology and Assessment

Earlobe defects result from traumatic injury, excessive tension on pierced earlobes from heavy earrings, or rarely from Mohs surgery for skin cancer. The earlobe is composed of skin, subcutaneous tissue, and fibrous tissue with minimal underlying structure. Assessment focuses on defect size and whether the defect is partial or complete (through-and-through).

Clinical Indications for Repair

  • Complete earlobe separation from trauma
  • Stretched or split earlobes from chronic earring tension
  • Cosmetic concern regarding ear appearance
  • Functional impairment such as inability to wear earrings
  • Large earlobe defects from excision of lesions

Repair Techniques

Primary Repair of Acute Lacerations

Complete or deep lacerations of the earlobe should be primarily repaired as soon as possible. After gentle cleaning, the laceration is closed with fine non-absorbable sutures (5-0 or 6-0) placed in careful interrupted fashion. The anterior and posterior aspects are closed separately if the laceration is full-thickness. Sutures are removed at 7-10 days.

Repair of Stretched or Split Earlobes

Stretched earlobes with thin edges or partial splits can be repaired through excision of redundant or scarred tissue followed by primary closure. The earlobe edge is carefully re-defined and the defect closed with fine sutures. The key is to restore the natural earlobe contour without tension that would promote re-stretching.

Complete Earlobe Reconstruction

Complete reconstruction of a missing earlobe is complex and rarely necessary, as most defects involve partial loss. When complete earlobe reconstruction is required, adjacent tissue is mobilized or tissue flaps are used. The goal is restoration of earlobe projection and contour.

Re-piercing Considerations

After earlobe repair, a new pierce location must be chosen to avoid the old scar. The new location should be in healthy tissue away from the repaired area. Re-piercing should be deferred until the repair is completely healed, typically at least 4-6 months.

Special Cases

Severe Stretching or Notching

Severely stretched earlobes with poor tissue quality may require tissue reduction and repositioning. Excess skin is excised, and the earlobe is reshaped. Multiple small excisions may be preferable to one large excision to maintain natural contour.

Traumatic Deficiency

Large traumatic defects may require advancement flaps from the neck or adjacent facial tissue. These flaps provide tissue with appropriate quality and quantity for reconstruction. Flap design must preserve the natural earlobe appearance.

Post-operative Care

Careful post-operative care is essential to optimize healing. The earlobe should be protected from trauma and tension. Patients should avoid wearing earrings until fully healed. Antibiotic ointment and careful cleansing prevent infection. Sutures are removed at 7-10 days.

Complications and Outcomes

Infection is uncommon with proper sterile technique. Dehiscence (separation) can occur if tension is excessive or if patient manipulation occurs. Hypertrophic scarring or keloid formation may occur, particularly in predisposed individuals. Most repairs achieve good aesthetic and functional outcomes with patient satisfaction high.
meta_description: Complete guide to earlobe repair including stretched earlobes, traumatic lacerations, and techniques for restoration.

title: Hidradenitis Suppurativa Surgery: Overview and Approach
content:
Hidradenitis Suppurativa: Disease Understanding

Hidradenitis suppurativa (HS) is a chronic, recurrent inflammatory condition affecting apocrine gland-bearing areas (axillae, groin, inframammary regions). The condition causes suppurative nodules, chronic draining sinus tracts, and scars. Medical management with retinoids, antibiotics, and biological agents provides initial treatment, but surgical intervention is often necessary for disease control and symptom relief.

Indications for Surgery

  • Recurrent or uncontrolled disease despite medical therapy
  • Chronic drainage with significant functional or quality-of-life impairment
  • Extensive sinus tract formation
  • Significant scarring and contracture
  • Individual lesion management (incision and drainage, deroofing)
  • Definitive treatment of localized disease areas

Surgical Stages and Disease Classification

Hurley Staging

HS is staged using the Hurley classification: Stage I involves solitary or few nodules without sinus tracts or scarring; Stage II involves recurrent nodules with minimal tract formation in isolated areas; Stage III involves extensive sinus tracts and scarring involving entire anatomical regions. Surgical approach is guided by disease stage.

Affected Anatomical Regions

The axilla is the most commonly affected site. The groin, inframammary fold, and perineal regions are also frequently involved. Less commonly, the buttocks, belt line, and other areas are affected. Assessment of all disease areas is essential before surgical planning.

Surgical Treatment Options

Incision and Drainage

Acute abscess formation requires incision and drainage for symptom relief and infection control. However, this is not definitive treatment and recurrence at the same site is typical.

Deroofing Procedure

Unroofing or deroofing removes the roof of sinus tracts while preserving surrounding tissue. This approach is discussed separately but is useful for localized sinus tract disease.

Wide Local Excision

Complete excision of all diseased tissue including sinus tracts, their ramifications, and affected surrounding tissue is the most effective surgical approach. This is discussed in detail separately but represents definitive treatment for localized disease areas.

Staged vs. Single-Stage Procedures

Very extensive disease may require staged procedures to manage the total surgical burden and healing requirements. Multiple staged procedures at 4-6 week intervals allow complete disease control while managing anesthesia exposure and surgical time.

Role of Imaging

Pre-operative imaging with MRI or ultrasound delineates sinus tract extent and ramifications, informing surgical planning. Imaging may identify previously undetected tracts that would otherwise be missed during surgery, leading to incomplete treatment and recurrence.

Post-operative Management

Post-operative care varies with the extent of surgery. Wound care is essential for proper healing. Some wounds require frequent dressing changes for extended periods. Pain management in the immediate post-operative period is important as HS surgery sites are sensitive areas subject to friction and moisture.

Outcomes and Recurrence

Wider excision and ablation of entire diseased areas with healing by primary closure or skin grafting provides the best long-term outcomes. However, recurrence can occur in 10-40% of cases even with complete excision, reflecting the systemic nature of HS disease. Continued medical management reduces recurrence risk.
meta_description: Overview of hidradenitis suppurativa surgical management including disease classification, indications, and surgical options.

title: HS Incision and Drainage: Acute Abscess Management
content:
Acute Abscess Formation in Hidradenitis Suppurativa

Acute abscess formation is a common manifestation of hidradenitis suppurativa, causing significant pain and functional impairment. Incision and drainage provides rapid symptom relief and infection control. However, incision and drainage alone does not address the underlying sinus tract disease and recurrence is common at the same site.

Patient Assessment and Diagnosis

Clinical examination identifies the location and extent of abscess formation. Ultrasound or MRI may delineate abscess size and identify associated sinus tracts. Assessment of systemic symptoms helps guide need for antibiotics and imaging. Culture of drainage material identifies causative organisms and guides antibiotic selection.

Incision and Drainage Technique

Preparation and Anesthesia

The site is cleaned and prepared with antiseptic solution. Local anesthesia with lidocaine and epinephrine is infiltrated around the abscess. For large abscesses or sensitive locations, conscious sedation or general anesthesia may be appropriate.

Incision Placement

The incision is made at the point of maximal fluctuance or at a natural site that follows anatomical planes. For axillary abscesses, the incision follows the direction of hair growth or natural skin creases. The incision should be large enough to allow adequate drainage and wound exploration.

Drainage and Wound Exploration

Once the abscess is entered, pus is expressed and sent for culture. The cavity is explored with a probe or finger to identify loculations and to ensure complete drainage. Blunt finger dissection breaks down loculations and ensures thorough drainage. The wall is not curetted or stripped, as this increases morbidity without improving outcomes.

Wound Management

The wound is left open to allow continued drainage and prevent re-accumulation. A sterile dressing is applied. Some practitioners place a drain to facilitate continued drainage, while others allow healing by secondary intention. Frequent dressing changes keep the wound clean and allow monitoring of drainage characteristics.

Post-operative Care

Dressing changes are performed daily or as needed depending on drainage volume. The wound is cleansed with sterile saline. Antibiotic ointment is applied and a fresh dressing placed. As drainage decreases, dressing frequency can be reduced. Patients should keep the area clean and dry between dressing changes.

Antibiotic Therapy

Culture results guide antibiotic selection. While awaiting culture, empiric antibiotics covering Staphylococcus and Streptococcus are typically started. Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole or doxycycline are common choices. Antibiotic duration is typically 7-10 days, though longer courses may be needed for extensive disease.

Recurrence and Definitive Treatment

Recurrence at the same site occurs in 40-60% of cases when incision and drainage is the only treatment. After resolution of acute symptoms, definitive surgical treatment such as deroofing or wide excision should be considered to prevent recurrence. Medical optimization with biologics or other agents also reduces recurrence risk.
meta_description: Management of acute HS abscess including incision and drainage technique, post-operative care, and prevention of recurrence.

title: HS Deroofing Procedure: Sinus Tract Management
content:
Deroofing as Intermediate HS Surgery

Deroofing, or unroofing, is an intermediate surgical procedure for hidradenitis suppurativa that removes the roof of sinus tracts while preserving surrounding healthy tissue. This approach bridges simple incision and drainage and wide excision, offering effective disease control while minimizing tissue loss and morbidity. Deroofing is particularly useful for early-stage disease or limited sinus tract involvement.

Patient Selection

Deroofing is ideal for patients with Stage I or limited Stage II disease with discrete sinus tracts. It is less suitable for extensive Stage II or Stage III disease with extensive branching tracts. Imaging with ultrasound or MRI helps delineate sinus tract extent and guides patient selection.

Pre-operative Planning

Careful pre-operative assessment determines sinus tract location, extent, and ramifications. Probing can outline tract course, though blind probing may miss branches. Imaging provides superior delineation of tract anatomy. Multiple sinus tracts in close proximity may require treating all connected tracts in a single procedure.

Deroofing Technique

Anesthesia and Field Preparation

Local anesthesia with epinephrine is infiltrated around the sinus tract area. For extensive disease or sensitive areas, conscious sedation is preferred. The area is cleansed and draped in sterile fashion.

Tract Identification and Deroofing

The sinus tract is identified by palpation, probing, or the presence of drainage. A surgical blade or cautery device is used to open the tract from its superior surface to its deepest point. The goal is to convert the sinus tract from a closed cavity into an open wound that can epithelialize from the base upward.

The roof of the tract is removed, exposing the tract cavity. Care is taken to preserve the tract walls and surrounding tissue. Granulation tissue and debris within the tract are gently debrided or allowed to remain as granulation tissue promotes healing.

Ramification Management

Sinus tracts frequently have branches or ramifications. These must be identified and deroofed as well. The probe technique helps outline branches. Complete deroofing of all tracts and branches in the involved area is essential to prevent recurrence.

Wound Management

Immediate Post-operative Care

After deroofing, the wound is typically left open to heal by secondary intention. Dressings keep the wound clean and absorb drainage. Some practitioners apply antibiotic ointment; others use simple sterile gauze dressings.

Ongoing Wound Care

Dressing changes are performed daily or as needed based on drainage. As the wound epithelializes, dressing frequency can be reduced. Healing typically requires 4-12 weeks depending on wound size and depth. Patients should keep the area clean and dry.

Healing and Epithelialization

The wound epithelializes from the base upward and from the edges inward. Healthy granulation tissue indicates proper healing. Secondary infection should be managed with antibiotics if symptoms develop. As the wound heals, drainage decreases and dressing needs diminish.

Recurrence Rates and Long-term Outcomes

Recurrence after deroofing varies with disease extent and follow-up duration, ranging from 10-40% in various series. Complete deroofing of all tracts and branches reduces recurrence. Continued medical management with antibiotics or biological agents reduces recurrence risk. If recurrence occurs, repeat deroofing or progression to wide excision may be necessary.

Advantages and Limitations

Advantages of deroofing include preservation of tissue and function, lower morbidity than wide excision, and faster healing for limited disease. Limitations include incomplete disease eradication in extensive tracts and higher recurrence rates compared to wide excision. The procedure requires careful technique and understanding of sinus tract anatomy.
meta_description: Comprehensive guide to HS deroofing procedure including technique, patient selection, wound management, and recurrence rates.

title: HS Wide Excision: Definitive Surgical Treatment
content:
Wide Excision as Definitive HS Surgery

Wide excision of all diseased tissue including sinus tracts, their ramifications, and affected surrounding skin and subcutaneous tissue represents the most effective surgical treatment for hidradenitis suppurativa. This approach provides the lowest recurrence rates and most durable disease control, though at the cost of larger defects requiring reconstruction.

Indications for Wide Excision

  • Extensive Stage II or Stage III disease
  • Recurrent disease after prior deroofing or incision and drainage
  • Multiple interconnected sinus tracts
  • Disease with significant functional impairment
  • Patient preference for definitive treatment
  • Disease unresponsive to medical therapy

Pre-operative Assessment

Imaging and Planning

Comprehensive imaging with MRI or ultrasound delineates the full extent of sinus tract disease. All branching patterns and extent are documented. This informs surgical planning regarding excision borders and reconstruction needs. Very extensive disease may require staged procedures.

Extent Definition

The excision includes all diseased tissue plus a margin (typically 0.5-1cm) of clinically normal tissue. For axillary disease, the entire affected axilla may need excision. For groin disease, the entire affected inguinal or genital area may be involved.

Wide Excision Technique

Anesthesia and Field Preparation

General anesthesia or conscious sedation with regional anesthesia is appropriate for extensive excision. The affected area is extensively prepped and draped. For axillary disease, the arm is positioned to provide optimal access and field visibility.

Excision Method

The excision is marked based on pre-operative planning. The incision is made around the affected area, following natural anatomical planes when possible. The diseased tissue is elevated as a specimen. Complete removal of all sinus tracts and their ramifications is essential. En bloc excision is preferable when possible, though the specimen should be carefully examined to ensure no disease is left behind.

Reconstruction Decision

Once the defect is created, a decision regarding reconstruction is made. Options include: primary closure with careful technique, healing by secondary intention with dressing care, skin grafting, or flap reconstruction depending on defect size and location.

Reconstruction Options

Primary Closure

For smaller defects, primary closure with careful tension management is possible. Intradermal sutures reduce tension on the skin surface. Multiple layers of closure improve durability. Tissue rearrangement with advancement or rotation may be necessary.

Skin Grafting

For medium to large defects, split-thickness skin grafts provide durable coverage with acceptable cosmetic results. The graft is sutured and secured with a tie-over bolster for 7-10 days. Graft take rates are generally high due to good blood supply of the underlying tissue.

Flap Reconstruction

For very large defects or defects in sensitive areas, local flaps such as advancement or rotation flaps can provide superior functional and cosmetic results. The blood supply must be maintained—axillary flaps are supplied by the axillary artery branches.

Post-operative Care

Post-operative care depends on the reconstruction method. Sutures or staples are removed at 2-3 weeks depending on location and healing. Skin grafts require immobilization and protection for 7-10 days. Wound care with dressing changes maintains cleanliness and monitors healing.

Pain Management

Wide excision can be painful post-operatively, particularly in the axilla and groin where movement and moisture are problematic. Adequate pain control with analgesics is essential. Compression garments can reduce swelling and provide comfort.

Outcomes and Recurrence

Recurrence rates after wide excision range from 5-30% depending on disease extent and follow-up duration. Complete excision of all diseased tissue minimizes recurrence. Continued medical management reduces recurrence risk further. Some patients may require staged procedures if disease extends beyond initially planned excision boundaries.

Return to Function

Full functional recovery takes 4-6 weeks depending on defect location and size. Axillary excision may limit arm mobility transiently. Groin excision may affect mobility and function. Progressive return to normal activities is typical as healing progresses.
meta_description: Complete guide to wide excision for HS including assessment, excision technique, reconstruction options, and long-term outcomes.

title: Pilonidal Cyst Surgery: Excision and Healing Options
content:
Pilonidal Disease: Overview and Etiology

Pilonidal cyst disease (pilonidal sinus disease) is a chronic condition involving the sacrococcygeal region, characterized by hair-containing sinus tracts or cysts. The condition results from follicular occlusion and foreign body reaction to ingrown hairs. While benign, pilonidal disease causes pain, drainage, and repeated infection requiring surgical management.

Indications for Surgery

  • Acute abscess formation with pain and infection
  • Recurrent drainage and symptomatic disease
  • Failed conservative management with antibiotics and depilation
  • Chronic intermittent pain limiting activity
  • Occupational or lifestyle factors necessitating disease resolution

Surgical Treatment Options

Incision and Drainage

For acute abscess, incision and drainage provides immediate symptom relief and infection control. However, this does not address the underlying sinus tract disease and recurrence is common. Incision and drainage should be followed by definitive surgical treatment when the acute infection resolves.

Sinus Tract Excision

Complete excision of the sinus tract(s) with margins is a standard approach. The tract(s) are identified and excised with a 0.5-1cm margin of normal tissue. The defect is then closed or managed depending on location and size.

Primary Closure Techniques

After excision of the sinus tract, the defect is closed with absorbable sutures in the subcutaneous layer and non-absorbable sutures in the skin. Meticulous closure with attention to tension distribution reduces hematoma and infection risk. The incision is typically oriented away from the midline to reduce hair-follicle contact with the scar.

Off-Midline Closure

Some surgeons perform off-midline closure, where the sinus tract is excised and the resultant defect is closed with the scar oriented laterally away from the natal cleft. This reduces friction and hair contact with the scar, potentially reducing recurrence.

Healing by Secondary Intention

For some patients, leaving the defect to heal by secondary intention after sinus tract excision is appropriate. The wound epithelializes gradually over 4-8 weeks. While this approach has longer healing times, it avoids the risk of dehiscence (separation) associated with primary closure. Careful wound care with frequent dressing changes is essential.

Cleft Lift Procedure

The cleft lift procedure excises the sinus tract and raises the skin and subcutaneous tissue of the natal cleft to reduce the depth of the natal cleft and its hair-trapping tendency. This approach reduces mechanical factors that promote disease.

Pre-operative Preparation

Hair removal from the surgical area is performed either pre-operatively or at the time of surgery. This reduces infection risk. Depilation, hair clipping, or even formal hair removal may be recommended. Thorough cleansing of the sacrococcygeal region ensures proper hygiene pre-operatively.

Post-operative Care

Suture Removal

Sutures are removed at 2-3 weeks depending on healing. Delayed removal in the sacrococcygeal region reduces tension-related separation.

Activity Restriction

Patients should avoid strenuous activity, prolonged sitting, and heavy lifting for 2-4 weeks post-operatively to reduce tension on the repair. Gradual return to normal activities prevents dehiscence.

Wound Care

The incision should be kept clean and dry. If sutures are absorbable, the patient may shower gently at 1-2 weeks post-operatively. If non-absorbable sutures are used, gentle cleansing and antibiotic ointment application maintain cleanliness.

Hair Removal and Hygiene

Long-term hair removal through depilation, shaving, or electrolysis reduces recurrence. Regular cleansing of the area reduces bacterial colonization and infection risk.

Complications and Recurrence

Infection and hematoma are common post-operative complications. Dehiscence (scar separation) can occur if tension is excessive or if patient activity is not restricted appropriately. Recurrence rates vary from 5-40% depending on surgical technique and post-operative hair removal compliance. Primary closure has lower recurrence rates than secondary intention healing in most studies, though healing time is shorter with secondary intention.
meta_description: Comprehensive guide to pilonidal cyst surgery including excision techniques, primary closure, secondary healing, and recurrence prevention.

title: Laser-Assisted Surgery in Dermatology: Applications and Technology
content:
Laser Technology in Dermatologic Surgery

Laser-assisted surgery encompasses various laser technologies that vaporize, ablate, or coagulate tissue with precision. The specific laser chosen depends on the target chromophore (melanin, hemoglobin, water) and desired tissue effect. Lasers provide excellent hemostasis, precise depth control, and reduced collateral damage compared to traditional surgical approaches.

Common Laser Types in Dermatology

CO2 Laser

The CO2 laser (wavelength 10,600 nm) is absorbed by water and creates thermal injury causing vaporization and carbonization. CO2 lasers are excellent for vaporizing tissue with minimal bleeding. Applications include removal of warts, benign skin lesions, scar revision, and resurfacing. Multiple passes allow precise depth control. The laser creates a slight char (carbonization) that is gently wiped away between passes.

Erbium YAG Laser

The Erbium YAG laser (wavelength 2,940 nm) is also water-absorbed but creates less thermal injury than CO2 lasers. This allows more precise ablation with reduced collateral heat damage. Erbium YAG is particularly useful for precise scar revision and fine wrinkle treatment. Healing time is often shorter than with CO2 laser treatment.

Pulsed Dye Laser

The pulsed dye laser (wavelength 585-595 nm) is selectively absorbed by hemoglobin, making it ideal for vascular lesions including port-wine stains, hemangiomas, and superficial vascular malformations. The laser creates selective photothermolysis of targeted blood vessels with minimal damage to surrounding tissue.

Nd:YAG Laser

The Nd:YAG laser (wavelength 1,064 nm) penetrates deeper into tissue than other common dermatologic lasers. It is useful for coagulation of deeper vascular lesions and for hair removal. The laser can be delivered through fiber-optic systems allowing flexible delivery.

Surgical Applications

Wart Removal

CO2 laser vaporization of warts is highly effective, particularly for resistant plantar and periungual warts. The laser vaporizes the wart tissue completely, with potential for histopathology of the ablated material. Recurrence rates are moderate but may be higher than traditional surgical excision.

Lesion Removal

CO2 laser can remove many benign skin lesions including moles, skin tags, syringomas, and other superficial growths. The laser ablates tissue with excellent hemostasis and minimal scarring for small lesions.

Scar Revision

Ablative lasers (CO2 or Erbium YAG) can improve scar appearance by removing the scar surface irregularities and inducing collagen remodeling. Multiple treatments may be necessary. Fractional lasers allow ablation of a fraction of the skin surface, reducing downtime compared to fully ablative treatment.

Nail Bed Surgery

CO2 or Nd:YAG lasers can be used to ablate nail matrix tissue as an alternative to chemical matricectomy. The laser provides precise destruction of targeted matrix tissue with less systemic absorption of chemical agents.

Safety Considerations

Eye Protection

All laser procedures require appropriate eye protection for the operator, staff, and patient. Wavelength-specific laser safety glasses must be worn. Non-laser workers should wear protective glasses if in the treatment area.

Smoke Evacuation

Laser ablation creates a smoke plume containing vaporized tissue and potentially viral particles. Appropriate smoke evacuation systems with HEPA filters reduce operator exposure and improve visibility.

Fire Safety

Laser use around oxygen and flammable materials requires precautions. Wet towels placed around the treatment area reduce fire risk. Oxygen concentrations should be minimized when possible.

Post-operative Care

Ablated areas are left as open wounds that epithelialize over days to weeks depending on depth. Antibiotic ointment and appropriate wound dressings protect the area and promote healing. Sun protection is essential to prevent post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation. Sutures are not typically needed unless the laser treatment is combined with surgical excision.

Advantages and Limitations

Advantages of laser-assisted surgery include excellent hemostasis, precise depth control, minimal collateral damage, and reduced scarring for small lesions. Limitations include cost of equipment, difficulty in achieving histopathology in some applications, and potential for post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation in darker skin types. Laser treatment requires appropriate training and understanding of laser physics.
meta_description: Comprehensive guide to laser-assisted dermatologic surgery including CO2, Erbium, dye, and YAG lasers with applications and safety.

title: Electrosurgery in Dermatology: Techniques and Applications
content:
Electrosurgery: Physics and Mechanism

Electrosurgery uses electrical current to cut, coagulate, or ablate tissue. The electrical current creates thermal energy within the tissue, causing cells to heat and either vaporize (cutting mode) or denature proteins (coagulation mode). Electrosurgery is versatile, cost-effective, and widely available, making it a staple of dermatologic surgery.

Types of Electrosurgical Current

Monopolar Current

Monopolar current flows from the electrosurgical unit through the patient via an active electrode (handpiece) to a dispersive electrode (ground pad). The current passes through the body between these two electrodes. Monopolar current is effective but requires a large dispersive electrode to prevent burns under the pad. This mode is primarily used for cutting (unmodulated high-frequency current) and coagulation (modulated current).

Bipolar Current

Bipolar current flows between two closely spaced electrodes on the handpiece, limiting current flow to the immediate tissue between the electrodes. Bipolar electrosurgery eliminates the need for a dispersive electrode and reduces risk of unwanted current flow through the body. Bipolar systems are safer for use on digits and sensitive areas.

Cutting vs. Coagulation

Cutting Mode

Unmodulated high-frequency current causes rapid tissue vaporization and separation. The active electrode does not contact tissue—an arc bridges the gap, creating the cutting effect. This provides rapid tissue division with hemostasis as a secondary benefit. True cutting requires careful technique to avoid collateral thermal damage.

Coagulation Mode

Modulated (intermittent) current causes tissue protein denaturation without vaporization. The electrode contacts tissue directly, heating it to coagulation temperature. This mode is excellent for hemostasis and is commonly used for lesion removal where vaporization of the entire lesion is desired.

Blend Mode

Some electrosurgical units offer a blend mode combining cutting and coagulation current. This provides simultaneous cutting with hemostasis but with more collateral thermal damage than pure cutting mode. Blend mode is useful when cutting larger vessels that would bleed excessively in pure cutting mode.

Clinical Applications

Wart Removal

Electrocautery combined with curettage effectively removes warts. The wart is first debrided with a dermal curette, removing the hyperkeratotic surface. Electrocautery then ablates remaining wart tissue and any remaining hyperkeratotic material.

Skin Tag Removal

Small skin tags can be removed by grasping the base with forceps and applying electrocautery to cauterize the base. The tag separates within days or is removed with minimal bleeding. Larger tags require excision combined with cautery for hemostasis.

Lesion Ablation
