Coagulation mode electrosurgery can ablate various benign skin lesions. The electrode tip contacts the lesion and tissue heating causes vaporization and ablation. Multiple passes may be necessary for complete lesion removal. The surrounding tissue is heated somewhat but generally heals well.

Hemostasis During Excision

Electrocautery provides excellent hemostasis during surgical excision. Small bleeders are touched with the electrode tip for a fraction of a second, cauterizing the vessel. This reduces operative bleeding and improves visibility.

Safety Considerations

Dispersive Electrode Placement

Proper placement of the dispersive electrode is critical for monopolar electrosurgery. The electrode should be placed on a large muscular surface area away from bony prominences, metal implants, and areas of poor blood supply. Inadequate pad contact can cause burns under the pad. Bipolar systems eliminate this risk.

Patient Selection

Patients with pacemakers or other electrical implants should not undergo monopolar electrosurgery, as the current may interfere with device function. Bipolar systems may be acceptable depending on the implant type—consultation with cardiologists is advised.

Smoke Evacuation

Electrosurgery creates a smoke plume containing vaporized tissue. Proper smoke evacuation reduces operator inhalation exposure and improves visibility.

Advantages and Disadvantages

Advantages include cost-effectiveness, availability, and versatility for multiple applications. Hemostasis is excellent. Disadvantages include thermal collateral damage potentially creating larger scars than laser surgery, inability to obtain histopathology of vaporized tissue, and patient discomfort from the burning smell and smoke. Care must be taken to control power settings to minimize collateral damage.
meta_description: Complete guide to electrosurgery in dermatology including monopolar, bipolar, cutting, coagulation modes and clinical applications.

title: Suture Techniques in Dermatologic Surgery: Principles and Practice
content:
Suture Selection and Wound Closure Principles

Appropriate suture selection and meticulous wound closure technique are fundamental to achieving optimal cosmetic and functional results in dermatologic surgery. The choice of suture material, suture size, and closure technique depends on wound characteristics, location, and desired cosmetic outcome.

Suture Material Classification

Absorbable Sutures

Absorbable sutures are broken down by the body over time and do not require removal. Absorbable sutures lose tensile strength at varying rates depending on the material. Synthetic absorbable sutures (Vicryl, Monocryl) maintain strength for 2-4 weeks and are absorbed over 2-3 months. Natural absorbable sutures (chromic gut) are absorbed faster but are less commonly used in dermatologic surgery due to less predictable absorption and higher tissue reactivity.

Absorbable sutures are ideal for deep layer closure where removal is impractical. Intradermal (subcuticular) closure with absorbable sutures reduces tension on skin edges and minimizes suture mark formation.

Non-absorbable Sutures

Non-absorbable sutures maintain tensile strength indefinitely and must be removed. Silk and monofilament nylon are commonly used in dermatologic surgery. Silk is comfortable to handle and handles well, but has higher tissue reactivity and higher infection rates compared to monofilament materials. Monofilament sutures (nylon, polypropylene) have lower tissue reactivity and infection rates but can be more difficult to handle and can be subject to needle damage causing splitting.

Suture Size Selection

Fine sutures (5-0 and 6-0) are used for facial skin closure where minimal scarring is desired. The smaller suture creates smaller holes in tissue and smaller suture marks. Coarser sutures (3-0 and 4-0) are used for thicker skin or areas where fine sutures would be difficult to handle. The general principle is to use the finest suture that can be safely handled in the location being repaired.

Closure Techniques

Single-Layer Closure

Simple skin closure without deep layer sutures is appropriate for shallow wounds with minimal tension. The epidermis and dermis are approximated with interrupted or running sutures. Approximation should be precise, with wound edges everted slightly to account for tissue contraction during healing. Eversion ensures that the scar remains at the same level as surrounding skin rather than depressed.

Interrupted Closure

Individual sutures placed at regular intervals (approximately 3-5mm apart) allow removal of a single suture if there is infection or excessive tension. Interrupted sutures are standard for most dermatologic surgery. The suture loop is tied with a square knot to prevent slipping. Each suture should be placed perpendicular to the wound edge with equal distance from the wound edge on both sides.

Running Closure

A continuous suture line provides faster wound closure than interrupted sutures but removal of a single suture affects the integrity of the entire line. Running closure is less commonly used in dermatology but can be appropriate for some straight wounds with minimal tension.

Two-Layer Closure

For many dermatologic procedures, two-layer closure optimizes cosmetic results. Deep layer closure with absorbable sutures (intradermal layer) relieves tension from the skin surface, reducing suture marks and promoting even wound edge approximation. The skin is then closed with fine non-absorbable sutures for precise edge alignment and removal at appropriate timing.

The deep layer sutures are placed in the dermis, taking care not to penetrate the epidermis (which would leave marks). Sutures should be placed perpendicular to the wound and spaced to distribute tension evenly. The knot is tied deep in the wound, away from the surface.

Running Subcuticular (Intradermal) Closure

Running subcuticular closure using absorbable suture is an elegant technique for skin closure that avoids surface suture marks. A single suture is placed in the intradermal layer in a running fashion from one end of the wound to the other. The suture can be retrieved at the end and tied, or the knot can be tied after the suture is placed. This technique reduces wound edge tension and produces the most cosmetically pleasing results for many wounds.

Special Techniques

Mattress Sutures

Horizontal mattress sutures (also called figure-of-8 sutures) pass through tissue deeper than simple sutures, providing greater strength and better wound edge eversion. These sutures are useful for wounds under tension or locations where maximal wound edge eversion is desired. However, mattress sutures leave larger suture marks and are less cosmetically favorable.

Vertical Mattress Sutures

Vertical mattress sutures are placed perpendicular to the wound, passing through deeper tissue and then back through more superficial tissue. These sutures provide excellent edge eversion and are useful for wounds under tension or on high-tension areas such as the back.

Wound Edge Approximation

Edge Eversion

Proper edge eversion is critical for optimal cosmetic results. Wound edges should be everted slightly so that the final scar is at the same level as surrounding skin. Eversion is achieved by inserting the needle perpendicular to the wound edge and suturing with adequate depth to engage the dermis.

Tension Distribution

Uneven tension distribution causes dimpling and poor alignment. Sutures should be spaced evenly, and tension should be distributed equally across all sutures. Deep layer closure helps distribute tension from the skin surface.

Suture Removal Timing

Non-absorbable sutures should be removed at appropriate intervals to minimize suture mark formation while maintaining adequate wound strength. Face: 5-7 days; scalp: 7-10 days; trunk and extremities: 10-14 days; high-tension areas: 14-21 days. Very fine sutures on the face may be removed at 5 days to minimize marks, while coarser sutures on the back may remain longer.

Common Complications

Infection can occur from improper sterilization or technique. Suture abscess (foreign body reaction to suture material) causes localized inflammation. Suture marks result from too-tight sutures or prolonged suture retention. These are avoided through proper suture selection, adequate spacing, appropriate tension, and timely removal.
meta_description: Complete guide to suture techniques in dermatologic surgery including materials, closure methods, edge eversion, and timing.

title: Local Anesthesia in Skin Surgery: Agents and Techniques
content:
Local Anesthetics: Overview and Selection

Local anesthetics block nerve conduction by inhibiting sodium channel activity in nerve fibers, preventing depolarization and action potential propagation. Different local anesthetic agents have varying potency, onset time, and duration of action. Appropriate selection and technique ensure patient comfort and optimal surgical conditions.

Common Local Anesthetic Agents

Lidocaine

Lidocaine is the most commonly used local anesthetic in dermatologic surgery. It has rapid onset (3-5 minutes), moderate duration of action (30-60 minutes), and good tissue penetration. Lidocaine is available as 1% or 2% solution. The maximum safe dose is 4.5 mg/kg without epinephrine and 7 mg/kg with epinephrine. Onset is too slow for topical application alone but excellent for infiltration and nerve blocks.

Epinephrine Addition

Adding epinephrine (adrenaline) to lidocaine causes vasoconstriction, reducing bleeding and extending duration of action. Epinephrine should not be used in areas with end-arterial blood supply (fingers, toes, ears, nose tip) due to risk of tissue necrosis from vasoconstriction. Typical concentration is 1:100,000 or 1:200,000.

Prilocaine

Prilocaine is similar to lidocaine but has slightly longer duration and more rapid onset. It carries a theoretical risk of methemoglobinemia at high doses but this is rarely clinically significant. Maximum dose is 6 mg/kg without epinephrine and 9 mg/kg with epinephrine.

Bupivacaine

Bupivacaine has slower onset (10-15 minutes) but much longer duration (4-8 hours). It provides prolonged post-operative anesthesia, reducing patient discomfort. However, slower onset means waiting time before surgery and cost is higher. Maximum dose is 2.25 mg/kg without epinephrine and 3 mg/kg with epinephrine.

Procaine

Procaine is rapid-acting but short-acting (30-45 minutes). It carries higher allergy risk than other agents and is rarely used in modern dermatologic surgery.

Topical Anesthetics

EMLA Cream

EMLA (Eutectic Mixture of Local Anesthetics) combines lidocaine and prilocaine in an emulsion. Applied under occlusion for 30-60 minutes, EMLA provides adequate topical anesthesia for superficial procedures. Onset is slower than infiltration but no needle sticks are required. Maximum dose is 10g for areas larger than 100cm².

Lidocaine Spray and Solutions

Topical lidocaine solutions (4%) or sprays provide rapid surface anesthesia useful for superficial procedures. However, penetration is limited without occlusion or chemical enhancers. Sprays are useful for cryotherapy or electrosurgery of small lesions.

Infiltration Techniques

Direct Infiltration

Direct infiltration of local anesthetic into or around the surgical area is the standard technique. The anesthetic should be infiltrated intradermally and subcutaneously to block pain fibers in the area. Slow injection with a small-gauge needle (27-30G) minimizes patient discomfort. The injection should be placed where it will provide block of sensory nerves serving the area.

Buffering Anesthetic

Adding sodium bicarbonate to local anesthetic increases pH, reducing acidity and discomfort on injection. Buffering also accelerates onset by 50-80% by allowing more anesthetic to exist in the non-ionized form. Typical ratio is 1mL of 8.4% sodium bicarbonate per 10mL of 1% lidocaine.

Warming Anesthetic

Warming local anesthetic to body temperature reduces injection discomfort. Anesthetic solutions can be warmed to 37°C before injection.

Nerve Block Techniques

Digital Block

Digital block provides anesthesia to the entire finger or toe by blocking the two digital nerves on either side of the digit. The anesthetic is injected on either side of the digit at the base of the digit or just proximal to it. Complete anesthesia is achieved with less total anesthetic volume than infiltration.

Supraorbital and Infraorbital Blocks

These blocks anesthetize the forehead and upper and lower eyelid regions respectively. Anesthetic is injected at the supraorbital or infraorbital foramen, blocking the branches of the trigeminal nerve (CN V). These techniques reduce anesthetic volume needed for significant facial areas.

Greater Occipital Nerve Block

This block anesthetizes the posterior scalp by injecting anesthetic around the greater occipital nerve as it emerges from between the trapezius and sternocleidomastoid muscles.

Safety Considerations

Dose Limits

Exceeding maximum recommended doses of local anesthetics risks systemic toxicity including cardiac arrhythmias, seizures, and cardiovascular collapse. Doses should be carefully calculated based on patient weight and the concentration of anesthetic used. Addition of epinephrine significantly increases maximum safe dose.

Allergy Considerations

True allergy to amide local anesthetics (lidocaine, prilocaine, bupivacaine) is rare but possible. Patients with documented allergy can usually use ester agents (procaine, benzocaine) as cross-reactivity is uncommon. If significant allergy history exists, testing with diluted anesthetic or non-anesthetic alternatives should be considered.

Epinephrine Contraindications

Epinephrine should not be used in areas of end-arterial blood supply. Additionally, epinephrine carries risk of cardiac effects in patients with significant cardiac disease, uncontrolled hypertension, or those taking certain medications (TCAs, some decongestants). Plain lidocaine without epinephrine should be used in these patients.

Post-operative Anesthesia Management

Extended anesthesia with bupivacaine provides post-operative pain relief, reducing patient discomfort. This can reduce or eliminate need for post-operative pain medications. Patients should be advised that the area will remain numb for several hours and they should avoid biting or traumatizing numb lips or mouth.
meta_description: Comprehensive guide to local anesthesia in skin surgery including agents, infiltration, nerve blocks, and safety considerations.

title: Tumescent Anesthesia: Infiltration Technique and Advantages
content:
Tumescent Anesthesia: Definition and Technique

Tumescent anesthesia is an infiltration technique that involves infusing large volumes of dilute local anesthetic solution (typically dilute lidocaine and epinephrine with saline and sodium bicarbonate) into subcutaneous tissue to achieve anesthesia, hemostasis, and expansion of the surgical field. The infusion creates a tumid (swollen) layer of tissue, hence the name.

Solution Composition

The typical tumescent solution contains 1000 mL normal saline, 1 mL of 1% lidocaine (10 mg), epinephrine 0.001% (0.1 mg), and 12.5 mL of 8.4% sodium bicarbonate. This creates a very dilute anesthetic solution suitable for large-volume infiltration.

Lidocaine in Tumescent Anesthesia

The dilute lidocaine concentration in tumescent solution (0.1%) is much lower than typical infiltration anesthetic (1%). However, the large volume infused provides sufficient anesthetic effect. Maximum recommended dose of lidocaine in tumescent anesthesia is approximately 55 mg/kg (compared to 7 mg/kg for conventional infiltration), allowing safe infiltration of very large areas.

Epinephrine Role

Epinephrine in the solution causes intense vasoconstriction, creating a bloodless surgical field and dramatically reducing bleeding. This allows visualization of deeper structures and reduces operative bleeding. Epinephrine also slows lidocaine absorption, extending duration and allowing higher total doses safely.

Sodium Bicarbonate

Sodium bicarbonate increases pH, reducing acidity of the solution. This allows more anesthetic to exist in the non-ionized, penetrating form. Buffering accelerates anesthetic onset significantly and reduces discomfort on injection.

Infiltration Technique

Solution Preparation

The solution should be prepared using sterile technique, adding components to normal saline in appropriate order and concentrations. Some surgeons purchase pre-made tumescent solutions from pharmaceutical suppliers. The solution is typically warmed to approximately 37°C before infiltration to reduce discomfort and accelerate onset.

Infiltration Method

The anesthetic solution is infiltrated using a small-gauge needle (typically 25-27G) or a small cannula. The infiltration is performed slowly, advancing the needle through the tissue while injecting to create a uniform anesthetic field. The tissue becomes visibly swollen and tumid, allowing visualization of the swelling indicating adequate infiltration.

Infiltration should be gentle and methodical, avoiding rapid injection which increases discomfort. Large volumes may require 20-30 minutes of infiltration time. The area being infiltrated should be massaged gently after infiltration to distribute the solution uniformly.

Waiting Time

After infiltration, 5-10 minutes should be allowed for anesthetic effect to fully develop and for vasoconstriction to become maximal. This waiting period is essential—operating before complete anesthetic effect results in inadequate anesthesia.

Clinical Applications

Liposuction and Lipoma Removal

Tumescent anesthesia is ideal for liposuction and removal of large lipomas. The dilute lidocaine allows infiltration of large areas without exceeding maximum safe doses. The vasoconstriction provides a bloodless surgical field and reduces operative complications. Tumescent anesthesia has become standard for liposuction procedures.

Extensive Surgical Procedures

Any extensive surgical procedure covering large areas (such as wide excision of HS or other conditions) can benefit from tumescent anesthesia. The large volume of solution infiltrates the entire surgical area and extends anesthesia into surrounding areas that may be encountered intraoperatively.

Hemostasis Enhancement

The vasoconstriction from epinephrine in tumescent solution is profound and extends anesthesia time significantly. Procedures can be performed in a relatively bloodless field, improving visualization and reducing operative bleeding complications.

Advantages of Tumescent Anesthesia

  • Allows infiltration of large surgical areas safely with standard lidocaine doses
  • Creates a bloodless field through intense vasoconstriction from epinephrine
  • Provides longer-duration anesthesia reducing post-operative pain
  • Can be performed with local anesthesia alone, avoiding general anesthesia risks
  • Reduces operative bleeding and blood loss
  • Improves visualization of surgical field
  • Less systemic absorption of lidocaine due to epinephrine effect

Potential Complications and Considerations

Fluid Overload

Large-volume infiltration can result in significant fluid absorption. Volumes greater than 4-5 liters may result in clinically significant fluid shifts. Monitoring for signs of fluid overload (peripheral edema, pulmonary edema, congestive heart failure) is important in patients with cardiac or renal disease.

Lidocaine Toxicity

Although tumescent anesthesia allows higher total lidocaine doses, systemic toxicity can still occur if maximum doses are exceeded. Careful calculation of total dose infused is essential. Signs of lidocaine toxicity include tinnitus, metallic taste, confusion, seizures, and cardiac arrhythmias.

Epinephrine Effects

The profound vasoconstriction from epinephrine carries theoretical risk of tissue necrosis or complications in patients with significant vascular disease. Patients should be assessed for peripheral vascular disease before tumescent procedures.

Post-operative Management

The extended anesthesia from tumescent solution provides substantial post-operative pain relief, often eliminating need for post-operative analgesics. Compression garments may be applied to reduce swelling and provide comfort. Patients should be advised to monitor for excessive swelling or signs of fluid overload in the days following surgery.
meta_description: Complete guide to tumescent anesthesia including solution composition, infiltration technique, applications in dermatologic surgery.
